sections.text: AP Calculus AB. Module Three — composite, implicit, and inverse derivatives. This is Unit Three, FUN three-point-one through three-point-six. This is where the derivative toolkit grows up. By the end you can differentiate almost any function the AP exam throws at you — nested, tangled, or inverted.

Picture a balloon inflating. Its volume depends on its radius. Its radius depends on time. So how fast is the volume growing? You multiply the rates. d V d t equals d V d r times d r d t. That product of nested rates has a name — it's called the chain rule, and it is the most useful single tool in all of calculus.

Three big moves today. One — the chain rule for any composite function, outside derivative times inside derivative. Two — implicit differentiation, for curves where y is tangled with x. Three — inverse functions and inverse trig derivatives, including arcsine, arccosine, and arctangent. This module unlocks most of the derivative problems on the AP exam.

A composite function is one function nested inside another — f of g of x. Think of it like an onion. The input x goes into the inner function g, the output u goes into the outer function f, and you get f of g of x. To differentiate, peel from the outside in.

Here it is — the chain rule, in both forms. Prime form: d d x of f of g of x equals f prime of g of x times g prime of x. Leibniz form: d y d x equals d y d u times d u d x, where u equals g of x. Plain English — derivative of the outside, evaluated at the inside, times the derivative of the inside. You'll use this dozens of times per AP exam.

Three quick examples. d d x of sine of three x squared. Outside derivative — cosine of three x squared. Inside derivative — six x. Multiply: cosine of three x squared times six x. d d x of two x plus one to the fifth equals five times two x plus one to the fourth, times two — which is ten times two x plus one to the fourth. And d d x of e to the x squared equals e to the x squared times two x. Outside, then inside.

Here is AP trap number one. Students write d d x of sine of x squared and stop at cosine of x squared. Wrong. You forgot the inside derivative. The right answer is cosine of x squared times two x. Always ask — what's nested inside, and what's its derivative?

Now look at a circle. x squared plus y squared equals twenty-five. This isn't a function of x — it fails the vertical line test. But every point on the circle still has a tangent slope. The point three-four sits on this circle, and there is clearly a tangent line through it. So how do we find d y d x without isolating y?

Three steps. Step one — differentiate both sides with respect to x, treating y as a function of x, so every y term picks up a d y d x via the chain rule. Step two — collect every d y d x term on one side. Step three — solve algebraically. On the circle: two x plus two y times d y d x equals zero, so d y d x equals minus x over y. Plug in three-four — slope is minus three-fourths.

Big trap. When you differentiate y squared, do not write just two y. Every y is secretly a function of x — so by the chain rule, y squared differentiates to two y times d y d x. Always give it its chain-rule tail. And watch your signs when you collect and isolate d y d x — one sign error sinks the whole problem.

Your turn. Find d y d x for the curve x squared plus x y plus y squared equals seven, using implicit differentiation. Heads up — you'll need the product rule on the x y term. Pause now. Solve it. Press play when you're ready.

Walking through it. Differentiate term by term. x squared gives two x. The x y term, by the product rule, gives one times y plus x times d y d x — that's y plus x d y d x. y squared gives two y times d y d x. And seven gives zero. So we have two x plus y plus x d y d x plus two y d y d x equals zero. Group the d y d x terms: x plus two y, times d y d x, equals minus the quantity two x plus y. Divide: d y d x equals minus two x plus y, all over x plus two y. One caveat — wherever x plus two y equals zero, the denominator vanishes, so the curve has a vertical tangent and d y d x is undefined there.

Inverse functions next. If g is the inverse of f, then g prime of b equals one over f prime of f inverse of b. Geometrically — graphs of f and its inverse reflect across the line y equals x, so corresponding tangent slopes are reciprocals. Two conditions matter. First, f has to be one-to-one — monotonic on the interval — so the inverse exists. Second, f prime of f inverse of b cannot be zero — otherwise f has a horizontal tangent and the inverse has a vertical one. Example: for f of x equals x cubed plus x plus one, f of zero is one, so f inverse of one is zero. f prime of x is three x squared plus one, so f prime of zero is one. Therefore f inverse prime of one equals one over one — just one.

Inverse trig derivatives. Memorize these. d d x of arcsine x equals one over the square root of one minus x squared. d d x of arccosine x is the negative of that. d d x of arctangent x equals one over one plus x squared. Be careful with the domain — arcsine the function is defined on the closed interval from minus one to one, but its derivative is only defined on the open interval, because at x equals plus or minus one the square root in the denominator vanishes. So arcsine has vertical tangents at the endpoints. Same for arccosine. Arctangent, on the other hand, is differentiable everywhere. And if the argument is u of x, chain in u prime.

AP trap number two — notation. When you see sine to the minus one of x on the AP exam, it means arcsine — the inverse function. It does not mean one over sine of x, which would be cosecant. Read the parentheses, read the context, and never read negative one in the exponent of a trig function as a reciprocal. It is always the inverse function unless told otherwise.

Higher-order derivatives are just derivatives of derivatives. f double prime is the derivative of f prime. f triple prime is the derivative of f double prime. In Leibniz: d squared y over d x squared, d cubed y over d x cubed. Quick polynomial: f of x equals x to the fourth minus two x squared plus five. f prime is four x cubed minus four x. f double prime is twelve x squared minus four. f triple prime is twenty-four x. Now the AP favorite — implicit second derivative. For x squared plus y squared equals twenty-five, we already have d y d x equals minus x over y. Differentiate again using the quotient rule, then substitute d y d x back in, and simplify using the original equation. You get d squared y over d x squared equals minus twenty-five over y cubed. The lesson — for any implicit second derivative, you must substitute the first derivative back in. We'll use f double prime for concavity and acceleration in Module Five.

Last move — selecting procedures. Look at the form of the expression and pick the right tool. Product? Product rule. Quotient? Quotient rule. Composite? Chain rule. Equation mixing x and y? Implicit. Inverse function value? The reciprocal formula. Arc-function? The inverse trig table. Here's the meta-skill — AP problems almost always nest two or three rules at once. Take d d x of x times sine of x squared. The outermost structure is a product — x times sine of x squared. So product rule first: one times sine of x squared plus x times the derivative of sine of x squared. That inner derivative needs the chain rule — cosine of x squared times two x. Final answer: sine of x squared plus two x squared times cosine of x squared. Identify the outermost structure first, then peel inward.

Recap. One — chain rule: outside derivative at the inside, times inside derivative. Two — implicit differentiation: every y picks up a d y d x, then collect and solve. Three — inverse-function rule: one over f prime of f inverse. Four — memorize the inverse trig derivatives, and remember sine to the minus one means arcsine, not the reciprocal. Five — higher-order derivatives are just iterated, and they're the seed of concavity and acceleration. Your assignment — twelve mixed problems in the Learn Portal, due before Module Four.

Four steps to actually master this module. One — read OpenStax Calculus Volume One, sections three-point-six through three-point-eight. Two — work through Khan Academy AP Calculus AB, Unit Three. Three — submit the GIIS Assignment M3 in your dashboard. Four — book a fifteen-minute advisor check-in if any implicit problem is fighting you. Next up — Module Four: contextual applications of differentiation. That's related rates, linear approximation, and L'Hôpital's rule.
